worth_it_explanation: The bid strategy decides how much LinkedIn pays per result and how hard it competes for each impression, so the choice between Maximum Delivery, Cost Cap, and Manual bidding sets the cost ceiling for the whole campaign. The check is quick. The bid strategy is visible on each campaign's settings in Campaign Manager, and the delivery and cost per result are in the same reporting view. A strategy that no longer matches the campaign's goal can let cost per lead drift upward while delivery still looks healthy, and the fix is a setting change rather than a rebuild.
intro: LinkedIn Campaign Manager offers three bid strategies. Maximum Delivery spends the full budget to win as many results as it can, Cost Cap holds the average cost per result near a target you set, and Manual bidding fixes the bid amount you are willing to pay. These checks confirm that the chosen strategy still fits the campaign's goal and that any cost guardrail is set and reviewed.

audit_checks.title: Maximum Delivery runs without a cost guardrail
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Maximum Delivery aims to spend the entire budget for the most results, with no ceiling on what each result costs. As auction competition rises, cost per lead can climb while the budget still delivers, so a campaign that looks healthy on spend may be paying well above its target without any setting flagging it.

educate.base:
Maximum Delivery is LinkedIn's automated bid strategy. It uses LinkedIn's machine learning to bid for you while aiming to spend the full daily or lifetime budget and return as many of the chosen key results as it can. Maximum Delivery campaigns are charged by impressions, and the strategy does not expose a bid field or a cost cap, so there is no place to tell it a ceiling for cost per result.

The tradeoff sits in that missing ceiling. Maximum Delivery optimizes for volume against the budget, not for a target cost per result. When auction competition rises or the audience tightens, the strategy keeps spending the budget, and the cost per lead or cost per click can drift above what the campaign was meant to pay. Spend and delivery still read as healthy, so the drift shows up in the cost-per-result column rather than in any warning.

Cost Cap and Manual bidding each add a bound that Maximum Delivery lacks. Cost Cap holds the average cost per key result near a figure you set and lets LinkedIn adjust bids underneath it. Manual bidding fixes the bid amount directly. You may want to confirm whether the campaign has a cost target it needs to hold to, and if it does, whether Maximum Delivery is the right fit or whether a Cost Cap or manual bid would keep efficiency in range.

A few signs point to Maximum Delivery being a poor fit for the campaign:
  • The campaign carries a target cost per lead or per acquisition that the team reports against
  • Cost per result has risen across recent weeks while spend held steady
  • The campaign competes in a tight or high-demand audience where auction prices move
You may want to weigh the goal of the campaign against the strategy. Maximum Delivery fits a budget-spend goal more than a fixed cost-per-result goal.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a considered-purchase or business-to-business (B2B) store, the conversion is often a high-value sale with a long path, so a target cost per acquisition usually matters more than spending the budget in full. You may want to consider Cost Cap so LinkedIn holds the average cost per result near that target rather than spending freely under Maximum Delivery.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-capture campaign, the cost per lead is usually the number the team is judged on, which is the case Maximum Delivery is least suited to since it carries no cost ceiling. You may want to consider Cost Cap set to the lead target, or a manual bid, so a rising auction does not push cost per lead past what the campaign can sustain.

learn_more.source_title: Campaign and ad set bidding strategies

investigate.intro: Confirm each campaign's bid strategy in Campaign Manager and check whether a campaign on Maximum Delivery has a cost-per-result target it needs to hold to.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager and select the account, then open the campaign group and the campaigns inside it.
Add or open the bid strategy column in the campaign table, or open a campaign's settings, and note which strategy each campaign uses.
For any campaign set to Maximum Delivery, open the reporting columns and read the cost per result over recent weeks. Note whether cost per lead or cost per click has risen while spend stayed level.
Compare the campaign's stated goal with the strategy. If the campaign reports against a target cost per result, record that Maximum Delivery carries no ceiling to hold that target.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the bid strategy and whether the campaign has a target cost per result. A campaign on Maximum Delivery with no cost target, or where spend is the goal, passes. A campaign on Maximum Delivery that reports against a target cost per result, or where cost per result has drifted above that target, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the cost trend.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the bid strategy and the cost-per-result target for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more of {{site_name}}'s campaigns run on Maximum Delivery without a cost guardrail. Maximum Delivery spends the full budget to win as many results as it can, with no ceiling on cost per result, so when auction competition rises the cost per lead can climb while spend and delivery still look healthy. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each affected campaign against its cost target and, where the campaign reports against a target cost per result, moving it to Cost Cap set near that target or to a manual bid that bounds what each result costs. After the change, watch the cost-per-result column to confirm efficiency holds within range.

audit_checks.title: Manual bids are set once and never adjusted to performance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A manual bid that is never revisited drifts away from the live auction. Set too low it starves delivery and the campaign underspends, set too high it overpays for results that a lower bid would still win. Either way the bid stops reflecting current competition, and the gap widens the longer it goes unreviewed.

educate.base:
Manual bidding lets you set the exact amount you are willing to pay per key result rather than handing the bid to LinkedIn's automation. It gives the most control over the bid value, and depending on the objective and ad format it may charge by impressions, clicks, sends, or video views. When you choose manual bidding, Campaign Manager shows a recommended bid range that estimates what other advertisers targeting the same audience are paying.

That recommended range moves over time. Audience demand shifts, competitors enter and leave the auction, and seasonal pressure changes what an impression costs. A manual bid entered once and left in place stops tracking those changes. The number stays fixed while the auction around it does not.

The two failure directions are opposite. A bid set too low relative to the current auction wins few impressions, so delivery slows and the campaign underspends its budget. A bid set too high pays more per result than the auction requires, so the campaign overspends for outcomes a lower bid would still have won. Both pull the campaign away from its efficiency target.

A few signs suggest a manual bid has gone stale:
  • The bid sits at or below the bottom of the current recommended range and delivery has slowed
  • The bid sits well above the recommended range with no record of why
  • The campaign has run for weeks or months with no logged change to the bid
You may want to compare each manual bid against the current recommended range and the campaign's recent delivery, then set a cadence to review it rather than leaving it fixed.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a considered-purchase or business-to-business (B2B) store, a stale manual bid that sits too low can quietly starve a campaign of the impressions it needs to reach a narrow buying audience. You may want to confirm the bid still falls within the recommended range and that delivery matches the budget the campaign was given.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-capture campaign, a manual bid left unchanged while the auction climbs either slows lead flow when it is too low or inflates cost per lead when it is too high. You may want to review the bid against the recommended range on a set cadence so the cost per lead stays in the range the campaign can sustain.

investigate.intro: Confirm which campaigns use manual bidding and check each manual bid against the current recommended range and the campaign's recent delivery.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager, select the account, and open the campaigns within each campaign group.
Note which campaigns use manual bidding, and for each one open the campaign's settings to read the current bid amount.
Compare the bid against the recommended bid range Campaign Manager shows for that campaign and audience. Note whether the bid sits below, within, or above the range.

Open the reporting columns and read recent delivery and cost per result. Note whether a low bid is slowing delivery or a high bid is paying more than the range suggests, and whether the bid has any logged change history.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per manual-bid campaign, the bid amount, its position relative to the current recommended range, and the recent delivery trend. A manual bid that sits within the recommended range and matches the campaign's delivery goal passes. A bid below the range with slowed delivery, or above the range with no recorded reason, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the direction of the gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the manual bid amount and its position against the recommended range for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more of {{site_name}}'s manual-bid campaigns carry a bid that has not been adjusted to the live auction. A manual bid set once drifts away from current competition, so a bid that is too low starves delivery and underspends the budget, while a bid that is too high overpays for results a lower bid would still have won. {{voice_recommend}} comparing each manual bid against Campaign Manager's current recommended range and the campaign's recent delivery, adjusting any bid that sits outside the range, and setting a regular review cadence so the bid keeps pace with the auction.
